title: Shape of sampling distributions for differences in sample proportions
description: Practice using the large count condition to determine when sampling distributions for differences in sample proportions are approximately normal.
topic_path: Sampling distributions for differences in sample proportions
Shape of sampling distributions for differences in sample proportions

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that $60\%$ of adults in district A support a new ballot measure, while $45\%$ of adults in district B support the same measure. Pollsters take an SRS of $200$ adults from district A and a separate SRS of $100$ adults from district B to see the difference between the sample proportions $(\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B})$.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{60\%}$ of adults in district A support a new ballot measure, while $\purpleD{45\%}$ of adults in district B support the same measure. Pollsters take an SRS of $\maroonE{200}$ adults from district A and a separate SRS of $\goldE{100}$ adults from district B..."

District | Support $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Don't $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
A | $\maroonE{200}(\blueE{0.60})=120 \, \checkmark$ | $\maroonE{200}(1-\blueE{0.60})=80 \, \checkmark$
 B | $\goldE{100}(\purpleD{0.45})=45 \, \checkmark$ | $\goldE{100}(1-\purpleD{0.45})=55 \, \checkmark$

All of these counts are at least $10$, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$ will be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$ will be approximately normal, because we expect $120$ successes and $80$ failures from district A, and $45$ and $55$ from district B, and all of these counts are at least $10$.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $60$ successes and $40$ failures from district A, and $45$ and $55$ from district B, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $120$ successes and $80$ failures from district A, and $45$ and $55$ from district B, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ failures in district A.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ successes in district B.

assessment_items.question_content:
A psychologist takes an SRS of $40$ people in their twenties and $60$ people in their fifties and surveys them about their anxieties. Suppose that $48\%$ of people in their twenties and $28\%$ of people in their fifties have anxiety about flying. The psychologist will then look at the difference $(\text{20's}-\text{50's})$ between the proportions of people with anxiety about flying in each sample.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{48\%}$ of people in their twenties and $\purpleD{28\%}$ of people in their fifties have anxiety about flying. A psychologist takes an SRS of $\maroonE{40}$ people in their twenties and $\goldE{60}$ people in their fifties...."

Age group | Anxiety $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | None $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Twenties | $\maroonE{40}(\blueE{0.48})=19.2 \, \checkmark$ | $\maroonE{40}(1-\blueE{0.48})=20.8 \, \checkmark$
 Fifties | $\goldE{60}(\purpleD{0.28})=16.8 \, \checkmark$ | $\goldE{60}(1-\purpleD{0.28})=43.2 \, \checkmark$

All of these counts are at least $10$, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{20's}-\hat{p}_\text{50's}$ will be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{20's}-\hat{p}_\text{50's}$ will be approximately normal, because we expect $19.2$ successes and $20.8$ failures from people in their twenties, and $16.8$ and $43.2$ from people in their fifties, and all of these counts are at least $10$.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $19.2$ successes and $20.8$ failures from people in their twenties, and $16.8$ and $43.2$ from people in their fifties, and all of these counts are at least $10$.

Approximately normal, because we expect $11.2$ successes and $28.8$ failures from people in their twenties, and $28.8$ and $31.2$ from people in their fifties, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ people in their twenties with anxiety about flying.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ people in their fifties with anxiety about flying.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of health researchers survey a SRS of $300$ teenagers, of whom 
$120$ have low weekly outdoor activity levels ($<5$ hours a week) and $180$ have high levels ($\geq 5$ hours a week). Suppose that $35\%$ of teenagers with low outdoor activity levels have allergies, and that $41\%$ of teenagers with high outdoor activity levels have allergies. The researchers want to see the difference between the sample proportions $(\hat{p}_\text{H}-\hat{p}_\text{L})$ of teenagers with allergies.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{H}-\hat{p}_\text{L}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{35\%}$ of teenagers with low outdoor activity levels have allergies, and that $\purpleD{41\%}$ of teenagers with high outdoor activity levels have allergies...$\maroonE{120}$ have low weekly outdoor activity levels and $\goldE{180}$ have high levels..."

Outdoor activity level | Allergies $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | No allergies $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Low | $\maroonE{120}(\blueE{0.35})=42 \, \checkmark$ | $\maroonE{120}(1-\blueE{0.35})=78 \, \checkmark$
High | $\goldE{180}(\purpleD{0.41})=73.8 \, \checkmark$ | $\goldE{180}(1-\purpleD{0.41})=106.2 \, \checkmark$

All of these counts are at least $10$, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{H}-\hat{p}_\text{L}$ will be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{H}-\hat{p}_\text{L}$ will be approximately normal, because we expect $42$ successes and $78$ failures from teenagers with low levels, and $73.8$ and $106.2$ from teenagers with high levels, and all of these counts are at least $10$.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $105$ successes and $195$ failures from teenagers with low levels, and $123$ and $177$ from teenagers with high levels, and all of these counts are at least $10$.

Approximately normal, because we expect $42$ successes and $78$ failures from teenagers with low levels, and $73.8$ and $106.2$ from teenagers with high levels, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ failures in both samples.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ successes in both samples.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company sells [[☃ definition 1]]. Suppose that $70\%$ of the first-time customers who order a vegetarian meal will order again, while $65\%$ of the first-time customers who order a gluten-free meal will order again. The company takes separate random samples of $50$ first-time customers who order each kind of meal. The company will then look at the difference $(\text{vegetarian}-\text{gluten free})$ between the proportions of customers who order again in each sample.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{70\%}$ of the first-time customers who order a vegetarian meal and $\purpleD{65\%}$ of the first-time customers who order a gluten-free meal will order again. The company takes separate random samples of $\maroonE{50}$ first-time customers who order each kind of meal...."

Customer meal order | Order again $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Not $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Vegetarian | $\maroonE{50}(\blueE{0.7})=35 \, \checkmark$ | $\maroonE{50}(1-\blueE{0.7})=15 \, \checkmark$ 
Gluten-free | $\maroonE{50}(\purpleD{0.65})=32.5 \, \checkmark$ | $\maroonE{50}(1-\purpleD{0.65})=17.5 \, \checkmark$

All of these counts are at least $10$, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{V}-\hat{p}_\text{G}$ will be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{V}-\hat{p}_\text{G}$ will be approximately normal, because we expect $35$ successes and $15$ failures from vegetarian orders, and $32.5$ and $17.5$ from gluten-free orders, and all of these counts are at least $10$.

options.definition: Pre-measured ingredients and instructions to make a meal

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $35$ successes and $15$ failures from vegetarian orders, and $32.5$ and $17.5$ from gluten-free orders, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $35$ successes and $35$ failures from vegetarian orders, and $32.5$ and $32.5$ from gluten-free orders, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ customers who order again in both samples.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ customers who do not order again in both samples.

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that $8\%$ of all cars produced at Plant A have a certain defect, and $5\%$ of all cars produced at Plant B have  this defect. A quality control manager takes separate random samples of $150$ cars from each plant. The manager will then look at the difference $(\text{A}-\text{B})$ between the proportions of cars with the defect in each sample.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{8\%}$ of all cars produced at Plant A have a certain defect, and $\purpleD{5\%}$ of all cars produced at Plant B have  this defect. A quality control manager takes separate random samples of $\maroonE{150}$ cars from each plant..."

Plant | Defective $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Not $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
A | $\maroonE{150}(\blueE{0.08})=12$ | $\maroonE{150}(1-\blueE{0.08})=138$
 B | $\maroonE{150}(\purpleD{0.05})=7.5$ ⚠️ | $\maroonE{150}(1-\purpleD{0.05})=142.5$

We don't expect at least $10$ defective cars from Plant B, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ defective cars in Plant B.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $12$ defective and $138$ defect-free cars from Plant A, and $15$ and $135$ from Plant B, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $16$ defective and $134$ defect-free cars from Plant A, and $10$ and $140$ from Plant B, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ defective cars in Plant A.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ defective cars in Plant B.

assessment_items.question_content:
The organizers of a mentoring program are curious about whether there are differences in outcomes when the gender of the mentor matches the young adult mentee. They take separate random samples of $80$ young adults with either the same-gender or different-gender mentors to see the difference between the sample proportions $(\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{D})$. 
Suppose that $90\%$ of young adults with same-gender mentors get jobs while $84\%$ different-gender mentors get jobs. 

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{D}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{90\%}$ of young adults with same-gender mentors get jobs while $\purpleD{84\%}$ different-gender mentors get jobs. They take separate random samples of $\maroonE{80}$ young adults with either the same-gender or different-gender mentors...."

Mentor | With job $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | No job $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Same-gender | $\maroonE{80}(\blueE{0.9})=72$ | $\maroonE{80}(1-\blueE{0.9})=8$ ⚠️
Different-gender | $\maroonE{80}(\purpleD{0.84})=67.2$ | $\maroonE{80}(1-\purpleD{0.84})=12.8$

We don't expect at least $10$ young adults without jobs with a same-gender mentor, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{D}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{S}-\hat{p}_\text{D}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ young adults without jobs with same-gender mentors.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $72$ with jobs from those with same-gender mentors, and $67.2$ from those with different-gender mentors, and both of these counts are at least $10$.

Approximately normal, because we expect $90$ with jobs and $10$ without jobs from those with same-gender mentors, and $84$ and $16$ from those with different-gender mentors, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ young adults without jobs with same-gender mentors.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ young adults without jobs with different-gender mentors.

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that $28\%$ of [[☃ definition 2]] adoptions are [[☃ definition 1]], and that $84\%$ of international adoptions are transracial in the US. A social worker takes a SRS of $200$ US adoptions, and in that sample, there are $160$ domestic adoptions and $40$ international adoptions. The social worker will then look at the difference $(\text{I}-\text{D})$ between the proportions of transracial adoptions in each sample.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{28\%}$ of domestic adoptions are transracial, and that $\purpleD{84\%}$ of international adoptions are transracial...There are $\maroonE{160}$ domestic adoptions and $\goldE{40}$ international adoptions..."

Type of adoption | Transracial $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Not $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Domestic | $\maroonE{160}(\blueE{0.28})=44.8$ | $\maroonE{160}(1-\blueE{0.28})=115.2$
International | $\goldE{40}(\purpleD{0.84})=33.6$ | $\goldE{40}(1-\purpleD{0.84})=6.4$ ⚠️

We don't expect at least $10$ non-transracial international adoptions, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{I}-\hat{p}_\text{D}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{I}-\hat{p}_\text{D}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ non-transracial international adoptions.

options.definition: Having parents with a different race or origin than the adopted child

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $11.2$ transracial and $28.8$ non-transracial domestic adoption, and $134.4$ and $25.6$ international adoptions, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $56$ transracial and $144$ non-transracial domestic adoption, and $134.4$ and $25.6$ international adoptions, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ non-transracial international adoptions.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ transracial domestic adoptions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant sends customers in their loyalty program a coupon for a free birthday dessert. Suppose that $8\%$ of the children and $12\%$ of the adults redeem their coupons.

The restaurant takes separate random samples of $100$ children and $90$ adults from their loyalty program to see the difference between the sample proportions $(\hat{p}_\text{C}-\hat{p}_\text{A})$. 

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{C}-\hat{p}_\text{A}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{8\%}$ of the children and $\purpleD{12\%}$ of the adults redeem their coupons. The restaurant takes separate random samples of $\maroonE{100}$ children and $\goldE{90}$ adults...."

Age group | Redeem coupon $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Not $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Child | $\maroonE{100}(\blueE{0.08})=8$ ⚠️| $\maroonE{100}(1-\blueE{0.08})=92$
Adult | $\goldE{90}(\purpleD{0.12})=10.8$ | $\goldE{90}(1-\purpleD{0.12})=79.2$

We don't expect at least $10$ children to redeem their coupons, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{C}-\hat{p}_\text{A}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{C}-\hat{p}_\text{A}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ children to redeem the coupon.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $80$ redemptions and $20$ non-redemptions from children, and $12$ and $78$ from adults, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $80$ redemptions and $92$ non-redemptions from children, and $12$ and $78$ from adults, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ children to redeem the coupon.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ adults to redeem the coupon.

assessment_items.question_content:
Karina owns a bakery where she orders both green apples and red apples to use in her baked goods. The supplier of the green apples says that $8\%$ of their green apples are bruised. The supplier of the red apples says that $6\%$ of their red apples are bruised. 

Each week, Karina orders $120$ of each type of apple, and she looks at the difference $(\text{Green}-\text{Red})$ between the proportions of apples with bruises in each order.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...$\blueE{8\%}$ of their green apples are bruised...$\purpleD{6\%}$ of their red apples are bruised...Karina orders $\maroonE{120}$ of each type of apple..."

Apple | Bruised $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Not $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Green | $\maroonE{120}(\blueE{0.08})=9.6$ ⚠️ | $\maroonE{120}(1-\blueE{0.08})=110.4$
Red | $\maroonE{120}(\purpleD{0.06})=7.2$ ⚠️ | $\maroonE{120}(1-\purpleD{0.06})=112.8$

We don't expect at least $10$ bruised apples from either order, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{G}-\hat{p}_\text{R}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{G}-\hat{p}_\text{R}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ bruised apples in both orders.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $10$ bruised and $110$ not-bruised in both orders, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $16$ bruised and $104$ not-bruised green apples, and $12$ and $108$ for the red apples, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ bruised apples in both orders.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ not-bruised apples in both orders.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large company is considering installing charging stations for electric cars in their company parking lots. Suppose that $1.5\%$ of employees at the main office have electric cars and $2\%$ of employees at the branch office have electric cars.

The company wonders whether there is greater need for the stations at the main office. They plan to take separate random samples of $100$ employees from the main office and $50$ employees from a branch office. Then they will look at the difference in the sample proportions $(\hat p_\text{M}-\hat p_\text{B})$.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{M}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...They plan to take separate random samples of $\maroonE{100}$ employees from the main office and $\goldE{50}$ employees from a branch office...$\blueE{1.5\%}$ of employees at the main office have electric cars and $\purpleD{2\%}$ of employees at the branch office have electric cars...."

Office | Electric $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Non-electric $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Main | $\maroonE{100}(\blueE{0.015})=1.5$ ⚠️ | $\maroonE{100}(1-\blueE{0.015})=98.5$
Branch | $\goldE{50}(\purpleD{0.02})=1$ ⚠️ | $\goldE{50}(1-\purpleD{0.02})=49$

We don't expect at least $10$ electric cars from either sample, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{M}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{M}-\hat{p}_\text{B}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ electric cars in both samples.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $11$ electric and $139$ non-electric cars at each office, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $15$ electric and $85$ non-electric cars at the main office, and $10$ and $40$ at the branch office, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ electric cars in both samples.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ non-electric cars in both samples.

assessment_items.question_content:
A vision insurance company selects a SRS of $300$ clients who wear contact lenses. They randomly assign $200$ clients to the experimental group to receive an app that promotes contact lens hygiene. The remaining $100$ clients form the control group. Suppose that without the app, $96\%$ of their clients with contact lenses would have healthy eyes for the year, and that with the app, $99\%$ would have healthy eyes.

After a year, the insurance company looks at the difference $(\text{Experimental}-\text{Control})$ between the proportions of clients with healthy eyes in each group.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample proportions, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...without the app, $\purpleD{96\%}$ of their clients with contact lenses would have healthy eyes...with the app, $\blueE{99\%}$ would have healthy eyes...they assign $\maroonE{200}$ clients to the experimental group...$\goldE{100}$ clients form the control group...."

Group | Healthy $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Not $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
Experimental | $\maroonE{200}(\blueE{0.99})=198$ | $\maroonE{200}(1-\blueE{0.99})=2$ ⚠️
Control | $\goldE{100}(\purpleD{0.96})=96$ | $\goldE{100}(1-\purpleD{0.96})=4$ ⚠️

We don't expect at least $10$ clients with not-healthy eyes from either group, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{E}-\hat{p}_\text{C}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{E}-\hat{p}_\text{C}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ clients with not-healthy eyes in both groups.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $288$ healthy and $12$ not-healthy in both groups, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $192$ healthy and $20$ not-healthy eyes for the experimental group, and $96$ and $40$ for the control group, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ clients with healthy eyes in both groups.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ clients with not-healthy eyes in both groups.

assessment_items.question_content:
A movie theater chain has lost business during a global health crisis. They are surveying a SRS of $70$ customers each month to understand what would make their customers feel safe enough to return. Suppose that in July, $87\%$ of customers wanted to order tickets digitally, and in August, $90\%$ of customers wanted to order tickets digitally.

They wonder whether the demand for digital tickets is changing, so they look at the difference in the sample proportions $(\hat p_\text{A}-\hat p_\text{J})$.

**What will be the shape of the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{J}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Large count condition for shape of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ 

The shape of a sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ depends on whether both samples pass the large counts condition:

If we expect at least $10$ successes and at least $10$ failures in *both* samples, then the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2$ will be approximately normal. In symbols:

$\begin{align}
n_1p_1\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_1\left(1-p_1\right)\geq10
\\\\
n_2p_2\geq10 \quad &\text{and} \quad n_2\left(1-p_2\right)\geq10
\end{align}
$

If one or more of these counts is less than $10$, then the sampling distribution won't be approximately normal.

###Expected counts

"...They are surveying a SRS of $\maroonE{70}$ customers each month....in July, $\blueE{87\%}$ of customers wanted to order tickets digitally, and in August, $\purpleD{90\%}$ of customers wanted to order tickets digitally...."

Month | Digital ticket orders $(n_ip_i\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$ | Not $(n_i(1-p_i)\stackrel{?}{\geq}10)$
:-: | -: | -:
July | $\maroonE{70}(\blueE{0.87})=60.9$ | $\maroonE{70}(1-\blueE{0.87})=9.1$ ⚠️
August | $\maroonE{70}(\purpleD{0.90})=63$ | $\maroonE{70}(1-\purpleD{0.90})=7$ ⚠️

We don't expect at least $10$ customers who *do not* want to order tickets digitally from either month, so the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{J}$ will **not** be approximately normal.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The sampling distribution of $\hat{p}_\text{A}-\hat{p}_\text{J}$ will not be normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ customers who do not want to order tickets digitally in both samples.

choices.content: Approximately normal, because we expect $60.9$ customers who want digital orders in July and $63$ in August, and both of these counts are at least $10$.
Approximately normal, because we expect $121.8$ customers who want digital orders and $18.2$ who don't in July, and $126$ and $126$ in August, and all of these counts are at least $10$.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ customers who want to order tickets digitally in both samples.
Not normal, because we expect fewer than $10$ customers who do not want to order tickets digitally in both samples.
